data.title: Boeing And Airbus Deliveries Weakness Show Critical Challenge: What's Going On?
data.description:
Boeing (NYSE:BA) and Airbus (EADSF) are both working on a multi-year trajectory to ramp up airplane production. While order collection has not been a problem, evident from the nearly 2,000 net orders collected in 2025, hiking production has been far more challenging, with both jet makers navigating their own set of challenges. In this report, I analyze the airplane orders and deliveries for January. As mentioned in prior analyses, we're uniquely positioned to provide value estimates on orders and deliveries to more closely reflect the actual benefit to the businesses.

Below, I have attached the explanation of the analysis methodology from a prior report:

Explanation Of Analysis Methodology For Airplane Orders And Deliveries

For this analysis, I use an internally developed tool available on The Aerospace Forum. I will be assessing the net orders and deliveries. For each, I will discuss the units ordered and delivered and the associated dollar value.

The net orders provide us with a view of demand. This is a reflection of factors such as pricing and availability. Boeing and Airbus have a total backlog of nearly 15,000 airplanes. So, currently, we're not viewing things through the scope of needing more orders to support or increase production. The fact is that the backlogs have to be brought down to healthier levels, and that can be achieved through higher deliveries. Deliveries are what matter, and that's also the area where we see that neither Boeing nor Airbus has been able to support demand. Assessing the deliveries gives us a view of how the recovery for both manufacturers is pacing. As we assess the broader in-year trends, this report considers the year-to-date figures rather than monthly figures on orders and deliveries.

Boeing Takes First Lead Of The Year

Boeing booked a total of 71 single-aisle orders compared to 48 for Airbus, while in the wide-body segment, Boeing booked 32 orders versus one order for Airbus. This resulted in a lead of 54 airplanes for Boeing in the net order tally, driven by 23 units in single aisle and 31 in the wide-body class.

Single-aisle orders accounted for nearly 80% of the total net orders, with the remaining 20% going to wide-body airplanes. Boeing’s lead is primarily driven by January orders for 20 Boeing 737 MAX airplanes from Air India and an order for 30 Boeing 787-10s from Delta. Airbus on the other hand, saw modest order inflow, with the largest order coming from Spring Airlines for 30 Airbus A320neo family airplanes.

In terms of net order value, Boeing took a $6.1 billion lead driven by $5 billion in higher net order inflow for wide-body airplanes and $1.1 billion in higher single-aisle orders.

Airbus And Boeing Book Net Airplane Orders Valued At Over $11.5 Billion

Based on a single month in the year-to-date set, there is not a lot we can conclude yet. What we do see is that Boeing and Airbus booked 152 orders combined, up 75% from a year ago. Airbus orders were mostly stable, with growth being driven by Boeing orders being roughly 3x last year’s January orders. While Airbus is not having an exceptional start, we note that order inflow was at the highest level since 2020. In terms of share in the net orders, Boeing had a 68% share compared to 32% for Airbus.

In terms of value, Boeing and Airbus received $11.5 billion in net orders, marking a 51% increase. Airbus's net order value decreased by over 50%, while Boeing’s net order value surged almost 320%, giving it a 77% share in the net order value. In 2020, Airbus started the year strongly as Boeing was still dealing with the Boeing 737 MAX crisis, allowing Airbus to scoop up orders. Interestingly, while net orders in terms of units are 45% lower than the 2020 high, the value is just 20% lower, reflecting the net order mix.

We note that 2023 was an outlier in terms of net orders, as Indian airlines placed substantial orders during the year. Both jet makers saw order value rise faster than net orders, and that is driven by a higher share of wide-body airplanes in the order mix.

Boeing Starts Stronger In A Seasonally Weak Month

January is always a slow start to the year. Jet makers push airplanes out in December to meet year-end objectives and obligations, and that leaves little to no inventory, as airplane production also slows during the holiday season. Boeing and Airbus delivered a total of 65 airplanes, with 55 single-aisle airplanes and 10 wide-body airplanes. Boeing topped Airbus in both categories with a 19-unit lead for single-aisle deliveries and an eight-unit lead for wide-body airplane deliveries, giving it a total lead of 27 units.

In terms of value, Boeing delivered $3.3 billion worth of airplanes compared to $1.1 billion for Airbus, giving it a $2.2 billion lead roughly equally divided between the single-aisle and wide-body categories.

Airbus And Boeing Airplane Production At An Unsettling Point

What we have been seeing and hearing is that demand is high and airplane production is recovering. The reality is that while airplane production is indeed recovering, the number of deliveries fell. That is driven by Airbus, which is facing engine delivery delays leading to a reduction of five units year on year in the delivery numbers, while Boeing is now almost exclusively relying on new-built airplane deliveries rather than deliveries from inventory of jets built in prior years. That led to Boeing’s deliveries being stable year-on-year and overall production falling by five units for both jet makers combined. So, Boeing is seeing stability and deliveries in line with pre-crisis numbers, while Airbus is really struggling to boost production, with deliveries at the lowest level in years. Boeing had a 71% share in the deliveries compared to 29% for Airbus.

In terms of value, we see that delivery value was stable year-on-year with a $4.4 billion delivery value, of which Boeing had a 75% share, leaving 25% for Airbus.

We note that Boeing is around 50 units behind on what the delivery trend of 2020-2023 would suggest, while Airbus trails by 4-7 units. While the start of the year is not an indication of the full year's performance, we see that airplane delivery volumes remained stable amid supply chain issues for Airbus and Boeing, now being more reliant on production rates rather than inventory deliveries.

Conclusion: January Shows The Struggle For Jet Makers

With a backlog of thousands of airplanes for both jet makers, I am not concerned about order inflow. The focus is more on deliveries, and those deliveries show that Boeing and Airbus are struggling significantly to increase production. For Airbus, that is driven by supply chain challenges, while Boeing no longer has a padding from deliveries from inventory like it had in 2025. It does show that for jet makers to meet demand, Airbus needs to get its supply chain in order while Boeing needs to execute on a sustainable production increase path. January is not a strong indicator for the full year, but we do see the challenges for both jet makers coming together. That puts the critical execution path for both jet makers in the coming months in the spotlight.

data.title: Green Thumb Industries Remains Undervalued Despite Record Q4-2025 Revenue
data.description:
Green Thumb Industries Inc. (GTBIF) reported strong financial results for Q4-2025, including record quarterly revenue and net income. The company benefited from the conversion of its Minnesota dispensaries to adult-use. The stock price is down 5% over the last year and down 36% since I covered the company last December on Seeking Alpha. I change my previous rating from a Buy to a Hold. The rescheduling of cannabis has still not been finalized. The cannabis sector remains weak, and stock prices are on the downtrend.

Q4-2025 Results

Green Thumb Industries reported $311.1 million in revenue, representing a 5.7% increase YoY and a 6.7% increase QoQ. The number represents a record high for quarterly revenue. The increase was due to retail sales in Minnesota. The company converted its medical shop to adult use since the state legalized recreational cannabis. Strong sales in Florida and New York also added to the increase. Revenue from retail sales was up 5% YoY, and revenue from consumer-packaged goods was down 1% YoY.

Gross profit was $141.3 million, a decrease of 10.6% YoY and a decrease of 1.9% QoQ. Gross margin was 45.43% versus 53.71% YoY and 49.43% QoQ. The decrease was due to ongoing price compressions in US cannabis markets.

The company reported a net income of $83.2 million versus a net income of $12.7 million YoY and a net income of $29.3 million QoQ. Cash and ST investments increased to $274.3 million. Cash flow from operations was $90.1 million. Current debt is $244.9 million.

Market consensus for Q1-2026 revenue is $297.79 million, an increase YoY and a decrease QoQ. The company confirmed in their earnings release that revenue will be lower due to seasonality.

Overall, Q4 results were strong, and the company maintains a strong balance sheet. There are some risks on the horizon. Although the company licensed its hemp-derived cannabis products to RYTHM (RYM), these products are set to be banned next year. It is unclear how this will affect Green Thumb Industries other income from the license. Otherwise, the company’s operations are robust, with 113 cannabis dispensaries across 14 states. These operations and the company’s brands and wholesale distribution remain strong and at low risk.

Revenue has increased, and the company reported a record high for Q4. Gross profits have decreased, and operating expenses have increased. The company consistently reports net income and cash flow from operations. Debt is in proportion to quarterly earnings. Total assets outweigh long-term liabilities. The company has a strong balance sheet. Green Thumb Industries has strong financials, but it is unclear what the next synergy will be for the company except for cannabis rescheduling.

The company and its stock price are undervalued. Total EV is higher than market cap. The stock price trades below its book value and NAV per share value. Trailing multipliers, like Total EV/Revenues, also imply undervaluation. Uncertainty in the cannabis sector will continue to keep the price on the downtrend. The stock price is undervalued, but the trend will continue due to weak investor sentiment. The price may drop further before we see higher price channels. Curaleaf and Green Thumb Industries lead the pack in terms of revenue and assets. Green Thumb Industries is the only company currently reporting net income. The other companies are reporting a net loss due to 280e taxes, which will change if cannabis is finally rescheduled. Overall, the cannabis sector remains weak, and the stock prices are undervalued across the board. All of the US MSOs have strong balance sheets and financial performance, but sector weakness is keeping the prices on the downtrend.

Investment Strategy And Risk

Green Thumb Industries is at low risk of poor financial performance. The company has a strong balance sheet and reports cash flow from operations. Debt is in proportion, and liquidity has increased. The risk surrounds uncertainty around cannabis legislation in the US and overall price compressions in the sector.

A Hold rating for the company comes with a low to moderate risk. The sector remains weak, and stock prices are on the downtrend. Rescheduling cannabis will change the trend, but there have been no further developments. As to Green Thumb Industries, there are not any current synergies that on their own will boost the stock price, in my view.

Conclusion

Green Thumb Industries reported strong Q4-2025 financial results. Revenue has hit a new all-time high. Minnesota adult-use cannabis sales are driving the results. The company is also enjoying the top spot nationally for some of its brands. Higher sales in Ohio, New York, and Florida are also pushing revenue higher. The biggest issue now is sector uncertainty over rescheduling. I change my rating to a Hold.

data.title: CGDV: The More Flexible Strategy Should Be Key In A More Challenging Economic Environment
data.description:
Investing has gotten harder in 2026. While basic index investing strategies produced historically strong results for most of the last decade, a more mixed current growth picture, with prices higher and more middle- to low-income consumers struggling, most updated economic estimates suggest that those kinds of unique returns will be harder to come by moving forward.

With institutions such as the IMF forecasting global growth to be at 3%-3.5% in 2026, Europe continuing to face economic weakness, and China entering a new phase of more moderate and slow expansion, investments with more nuanced investing strategies are looking more likely to produce better financial results.

One ETF that has successfully used this kind of flexible approach to continue to consistently outperform the broader indexes is the Capital Group Dividend Value ETF (CGDV).

The Capital Group Dividend Value ETF has offered investors total returns of 89.39% over the last 3 years, while the S&P 500 (SPY) has offered investors total returns of 75.11 percent. CGDV has outperformed the S&P 500 on a 1-year, 3-year, and 5-year timeframe.

I last wrote about the Capital Group Dividend Value ETF in April of 2025, and I rated the fund a buy. I am upgrading this investment to a strong buy today. CGDV. Recent economic data shows a mixed growth picture, some stubbornly high inflation metrics suggest aggressive rate cuts are increasingly unlikely, and geopolitical uncertainty remains very high. This fund's more targeted approach that focuses on both stronger sectors and better-performing individual companies should continue to offer investors better value than many simpler, more broad-based investing strategies focused on the indexes.

The Capital Group Dividend Value ETF has an expense ratio of 0.33%, a current yield of 1.26%, and $30.52 billion in assets under management. The fund is invested 26.53% in technology, 15.37% in the industrials, 13.11% in health care, 8.85% in consumer cyclicals, 6.58% in consumer defensives, 6.34% in energy, 5.51% in communication, 5.15% in financials, 4.90% in basic materials, 2.67% in the utilities, and 1.74% in real estate. The four largest holdings of CGDV are Microsoft Corporation (MSFT), NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA), RTX Corporation (RTX), and Broadcom Inc. (AVGO). These positions make up a combined nearly 19% of this investment.

The Capital Group Dividend Value ETF officially reconstitutes twice a year and rebalances periodically at the fund's discretion, but this investment also, at times, changes holdings throughout the year. CGDV's fund managers have significant flexibility in deciding what positions to have and how to adjust the percentage of the portfolio these investments comprise. No single equity holding of CGDV is more than 5.34%; this is a well-diversified fund.

The Capital Group Dividend Value ETF seeks to pick high-quality companies with a history of strong cash flow, and the investment allocates capital 90% to US equities, with nearly all the holdings being large-cap corporations.

While CGDV's prospectus claims the fund targets funds with an average dividend yield higher than the average US stock, this ETF has never had a full-year yield of over 2%. This is a growth investment.

CGDV has outperformed both the S&P 500 and most of the fund's peers primarily because of the fund's overweight position in large-cap and also because of the investment's focus on higher-quality companies with stronger cash flow.

The Capital Group Dividend Value ETF also takes a markedly different approach to capital allocation than other funds with significant tech holdings, such as the Schwab Large-Cap Growth ETF (SCHG) and the Invesco S&P 500 Momentum ETF (SPMO). Since this investment limits holdings to at or near 5 percent of the exchange-traded fund's overall portfolio in addition to focusing on higher-quality names within this sector. CGDV did not hold Oracle Corporation (ORCL), one of the worst-performing large-cap tech companies over the last year; the fund's more nuanced selection process led the ETF's managers to avoid the software operator. CGDV has far less concentrated holdings in the tech sector than most peers, and the ETF's focus on cash flow and the recent performance of management teams has worked. With the growth picture more uncertain and several tech companies, including Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) and Oracle, recently missing earnings expectations by a significant margin, CGDV's strategy of having a more diversified portfolio within industries should produce better results moving forward as well.

CGDV's strategy of focusing on dividends and cash flow is also why the ETF has held some of the best-performing companies in the market over the last several years, such as General Electric Company (GE), Philip Morris International Inc. (PM), and British American Tobacco p.l.c. (BTI), and Royal Caribbean Cruises Ltd. (RCL). Corporations such as General Electric and Philip Morris International have been two of the best-performing corporations in the market over the last 2 years, and this ETF has consistently done a good job selecting some of the strongest-performing industries and companies in the market. General Electric and Philip Morris are both industry leaders that have significantly outperformed the market in the last several years. General Electric is in the middle of an extended secular growth market in the aerospace industry, while Philip Morris International continues to dominate the fast-growing and higher-margin alternative smoking industry. Royal Caribbean also remains the premium operator in the cruise industry, and the company is well-positioned to benefit from recent economic reports showing spending continues to hold up very well at the top end, since the cruise operator focuses on luxury vacation offerings. CGDV's dividend-based approach of targeting companies continuing to generate impressive cash flow even in the more challenging current financial environment should keep this ETF focused on both the best-run and stronger-performing companies in an economic situation where some corporations are likely to struggle.

This Capital Group Dividend Value ETF has even offered investors better returns over the last 5 years than even some of the better-performing large-cap funds, such as the Schwab Large-Cap Growth ETF ((SCHG)). The investment has also recently outperformed the Invesco S&P 500 Momentum ETF (SPMO) as well.

CGDV has outperformed SCHG by nearly 16% since this fund's inception in February of 2022. The Schwab Large-Cap Growth ETF tracks the Dow Jones U.S. Large-Cap Growth Total Stock Market Index, so this investment does not use the same discretion that CGDV does when picking companies.

CGDV has also significantly outperformed SPMO more recently, over the last six months, even though this investment has performed better than the Capital Group Dividend Value ETF over the last 4 years.

While the Invesco S&P 500 Momentum ETF focuses on total returns and momentum, this investment is still a large-cap growth fund that holds a number of the same core holdings as CGDV, such as Meta Platforms, Inc. (META), General Electric, and Broadcom.

SPMO uses a specific formula to calculate momentum and rebalance the fund's portfolio semi-annually that attempts to identify companies most likely to outperform from the S&P 500 momentum index. While SPMO performed very well over the long term, CGDV was able to avoid some struggling equity holdings that the fund's Invesco peer held, such as Oracle (ORCL), since this ETF uses a more nuanced approach to targeting investments. This is also why this exchange-traded fund has just 3.4 percent of the ETF's holdings in Europe, a region seeing more pronounced economic weakness, while nearly 90.5% of the positions of CGDV are in the US, which remains the strongest major economy. Dividend investing strategies have also worked better more recently than the approach of funds such as SPMO of targeting momentum, primarily because the economic data and earnings reports continue to be more fluid and harder to predict in what is an increasingly uncertain economic environment.

The recent financial data remains mixed, and the market outlook remains uncertain, with geopolitical tensions rising, the possibility of continued trade conflicts still high, and recent inflation data showing the Producer Price Index rose more than expected. The IMF is projecting global growth to be 3.3% in 2026 and 3.2% in 2027. Still, the Fed has seen a recent deterioration in consumer spending by lower-income families, and economic estimates for Europe remain weak, with some analysts forecasting growth in this region to be just above 1 percent. Companies such as McDonald's Corporation (MCD) and Walmart Inc. (WMT) have also seen a recent slowdown in spending at the lower end. The Fed has also observed that spending at the high end has not slowed at all. Top-end spenders made up more of overall consumption in the US at any time than since this data began to be tracked in 1989.

This kind of more complicated financial outlook should give funds such as CGDV that target individual companies that are outperforming in the current, more nuanced economic environment and use more discretion as an advantage. With growth expected to be less broad-based, narrowing the industries and companies most investors are allocating capital to should also make more sense, and The Capital Group Dividend Value ETF's approach of focusing on higher-quality corporations with stronger cash flow is likely to separate this fund's performance from the more formulaic approach many of CGDV's peers are using. This more aggressive growth investment should fit well in portfolios that are seeking to outperform the broader indexes.

All investments have risk, and the Capital Group Dividend Value ETF has not had to navigate a prolonged and more severe sell-off since its inception date in February 2022. CGDV's more narrow approach to investing means the fund is less likely to benefit from an improving macro-outlook than more broad-based index investments that have significant positions in some of the weaker-performing industries, such as consumer cyclicals. CGDV also has minimal holdings in the energy and basic materials sectors. This ETF is not likely to be well-positioned right now if inflation were to accelerate significantly in the near term.

The Capital Group Dividend Value ETF is one of the few investment funds that has consistently and significantly outperformed the S&P 500 over the last 4 years, and this investment fund's more narrowly tailored strategy of selecting companies should position this exchange-traded fund well in an economy where growth is increasingly concentrated in sectors such as technology and health care. While index investing may continue to produce strong results, the wider discretion CGDV uses to allocate capital should be an effective approach in the current more challenging market environment.

The consensus growth outlook remains positive despite the more complicated geopolitical and economic environment that is expected by most analysts moving forward. Growth funds such as CGDV should still be able to offer investors consistent solid returns of 8-10% if the current financial outlook of many economists that calls for global growth of nearly 3% this year holds up. While CGDV won't likely be able to offer investors the same historically strong returns seen in the last decade, most investors should still find this investment appealing.

data.title: Dine Brands: Applebee's Playbook Looks Familiar
data.description:
It seems that my last 'Hold' rating on Dine Brands (DIN) has aged well.

I was very happy to see during FY 2025 the change in Applebee's approach, stopping the 'endless LTOs' and switching to offering fixed-price promotions (both with the 'Really Big Meal Deal' and the '2 for $25').

This generated traffic and turned same-store sales positive. But that wasn't what caused the bull run.

That's when the catalyst for dual-branded openings appeared.

I gave you all the numbers that these prototypes can generate in my last article here (and I'll tell you upfront, they're much more economical than standalone locations).

As they project 50 openings in all of FY 2026 (~80 by the end of the year), the goal is still far from being achieved, and aside from renovations of company-owned stores, they depend on franchisees to carry out these renovations.

Out of the 30-plus openings, 21 are concentrated in Texas and Wisconsin (mainly with Hakim Group and Flynn Group). Not every franchisee seems to be racing to build these out.

A reader made a very interesting list about the updated new openings. I recommend taking a look at it.

Since the upside above my previous price target (mid-$30s) was entirely supported by these dual-branded locations, I decided to downgrade to 'Hold' again.

I figured the market wouldn't push above my target yet until the dual-branded showed up so much in the consolidated numbers.

At this point, we're sitting at a total return of 45% since my last 'Buy' rating, far outperforming the S&P, which has delivered only 5.9% since August of last year.

Not bad, if you ask me.

Almost three months after my last update on Dine Brands, I'd like to give my two cents on what's happening and finally update the thesis.

Some Tweaks I Liked

Instead of launching menu innovations within some kind of seasonal LTO or something like that, Applebee's is including them within the '2 for $25' offer.

That's exactly what I'd like to see in environments like this, even more so with so many price-sensitive guests out there.

This reminds me of Chili's late turnaround.

Brinker started by managing turnover in both the back-of-house and front-of-house operations. Then they launched the '3-for-Me' offer, offering both barbell and value. Only after all that did they make the 'Triple Dipper' go viral and explode sales in the same stores.

I'm not saying this is Applebee's exact path, but you can observe a move towards a Chili's-like approach after they launched the 'Grilled Cheese Cheeseburger' (a patty melt and grilled cheese hybrid) in October of last year.

Then in January, they rolled out the 'O-M-Cheese Burger'. This is a hamburger cut in half with lots of cheese and clearly made to be TikTok-worthy.

And, even though we're in the GLP-1 era, with FSRs like Cheesecake Factory (CAKE) serving bowls and battling with fast-casuals, it ended up working out for Applebee's.

The O-M-Cheese Burger became the chain's best-selling burger of all time.

Now think about it. By incorporating both into the '2 for $25' value architecture (1 appetizer + 2 entrées), you can generate traffic and gain buzz simultaneously from both ends of the barbell spectrum.

Looking at restaurant math, $12.50 per guest is smart if:

  1. It didn't have a very high adoption rate. In the case of Applebee's, ~22% of transactions in Q4 FY 2025 involved the deal, which seems sustainable to me;
  2. Pasta and salad (such as Chicken Parmesan Fettuccine and Oriental Chicken Salad) subsidize premium burger offerings, as they have a low cost and slightly higher margins; or
  3. Cross-selling beverages, preferably alcoholic ones, is a good option as they sell well on game days.

So, it seems to me the strategy is to lure in extreme indulgence (lots of calories per dollar) and increase the average check through add-ons.

But if you look at the industry, they're not the only ones doing this.

Red Robin (RRGB) also added signature salmon dishes within their Big YUMMM Deals for $9.99 with bottomless sides and drinks. And Denny's—now privately held—also integrated several new dishes into their all-day deals starting at $5.99.

We're certainly seeing a reaction from FSRs after fast-food chains took a chunk of traffic from June/July, especially from lower-income guests.

Whether this will work or not, that belongs to the future.

As Applebee's new burger is the best-selling in the chain's history and management said traffic returned to positive territory in January—even with storms across the country—I think this is a positive sign.

A Closer Look at Same-Store Sales

I don't know if you read my last write-up on restaurants, but Q4 was a terrible month for restaurants in the United States.

We're seeing a mix of an extremely sluggish labor market, still-sticky inflation, and a drop in real worker earnings. Add to that inflation in high-value supermarket items like coffee and meat, and you have weak consumer confidence.

Low confidence is certainly hurting discretionary spending and, consequently, restaurants.

Take a look at the NRA's Restaurant Performance Index.

The indicator reached 99.3 points in December after a stagnant October and November. The biggest culprit was traffic, which plummeted from 98 to 96.8 points.

This reading is very much in line with what the macro market had been telling us.

But there was a catch here.

See what I said in my First Watch (FWRG) write-up:

Despite all this, I still think there is pent-up demand. Guests want to return to restaurants and should do so when conditions improve (...) Conference Board reported that, despite worsening conditions, Restaurants, Bars, and Takeout is the spending category that consumers most expect to spend more on in the next six months

It seems that this pent-up demand from December showed up a bit in January. Despite the storms.

Both consumer confidence and RPI grew last month, which also makes sense given management's comments regarding the start of Q1. Now, we're left wondering if this was just a reflux from a terrible January or if it's a new upward trend.

But I also have another trick question (you must be tired of them by now, right?).

Although same-store sales in the RPI increased 1.5% from 98.3 to 99.8, traffic remained at 98.1 points. Even though the RPI indicates that the situation is better in January than in all the months of Q4, this isn't coming from a very large increase in traffic.

Of course, traffic improved compared to December, but I mean that compared to the other months of Q4 it seems flat to me.

The dormant demand I mentioned earlier also appears in the February consumer confidence index, which makes me think that this entire drop in traffic is something more macro-driven, not structural.

Speaking specifically about Dine Brands, here's an overview of same-store sales for Applebee's versus other casual dining restaurants:

And now on to IHOP with other family-dining chains:

Notice that IHOP saw positive same-store sales for the first time after seven negative quarters.

The 'House Faves' (approximately ~20% of total orders in Q4 FY 2025) are now driving traffic every day of the week, but I think the case here is more about soft comparisons. Even so, they outperformed the average family-dining restaurant according to Black Box Intelligence's measurement, as the CEO stated.

Looking ahead, I expect same-store sales to remain flat to slightly positive in Q1 for both IHOP and Applebee's.

With a still weak but normalized scenario, I imagine price-sensitive guests will return to cheaper FSR options even with fast-food chains competing on price at the lower end.

What to Expect in 2026?

Management released a rather conservative guidance for FY 2026.

As I said, this won't be a year of complete inflection for the brands, but more of a consolidation and possible normalization of traffic from the value platforms (especially in H2 FY 2026).

One downside is that they still expect some net closures, both at IHOP and Applebee's.

Same-store sales should be between 0 and 2% for both brands. Here, Applebee's is likely seeing a larger influx of traffic, and IHOP is still facing easier comparisons.

For EBITDA, they are targeting $220 to $230 million (an EBITDA margin of 24.3% to 25.4% for the whole year if we consider Wall St.'s projected revenue of $904.06 million, above the 21% of FY 2025 and very close to the 5-year average of 24.6%) and a Capex of $25 to $35 million.

With this data in hand and considering an EBITDA-to-OCF conversion rate of 50% to 60% (range of the last three years), OCF should come in at $123.7 million at midpoint.

This is enough to cover Capex and still generate approximately $93.7 million in FCF.

I also think that both the dividends ($10.1 million annually from a payout of $0.76 per share) and the share buybacks (at least more than $50 million if they follow the FY 2025 pace) are also well covered.

Speaking of buybacks, they still have ~$73.2 million authorized. I think $50 million is a really interesting number here, and if they were to carry it out with an average buyback price of $30, they would repurchase and retire 1.6 million shares (buyback yield of ~12%).

Adding it all up, Dine Brands' potential shareholder yield for FY 2026 could be just under 15%. That seems strong to me.

Now, regarding the debt, I don't think we'll see major repayments in FY 2026.

Since the market expects them to repay the notes around FY 2029 or FY 2030, I imagine they would focus on reducing, revolving, or repurchasing only notes that are trading at a discount. Covenants like maintaining DSCR at a minimum of 1.75x and Debt/EBITDA below 5.25x are well covered and, in my opinion, well protected unless some kind of recession occurs.

Still Fully Priced?

Wall St. made a small tweak to the price target, and now the consensus price target is slightly below mine ($31.40 per share).

Some estimates have changed and I want to take another look at the valuation before committing to any rating.

Looking at where I stand, with a projected midpoint EBITDA of $225 million and trading at an EV of $1.87 billion, the stock is trading at an EV/EBITDA of ~8.3x. I can also look at my estimate of OCF ($123.7 million) and divide it by 11.7 million shares outstanding post-buybacks, giving us an OCF per share of $10.57. Only 2.9x the OCF.

I imagine the fair range will continue to be typically for a low-growth FSR (8 to 10x EBITDA), and I still see Dine Brands as very close to my low-$30s price target.

But let's look at the P/E ratio. For example, if they report $904.06 million in revenue and a net margin between 5% and 6% (without considering possible impairments), would arrive at an EPS between $4.25 and $4.75. This upper end represents the market consensus for EPS.

Plugging in the 5-year average P/E and assuming margins move in that direction, I would have a price target between low-to-mid $30s.

Even if we look at the current FCF yield (~5%), Dine Brands are between the 5.5% of the penultimate article and the 4.5% of the last one. It's not as deep value as before.

Brinker, for example, trades at a FCF yield of over 5.7% and seems like a better 'Buy' than Dine Brands at these levels.

Since we're already sitting on a large upside, I prefer 'Hold' here. Sit on the upside and relax collecting dividends along the way.

I think Applebee's could outperform the industry in FY 2026 and that dual-branded locations could extend the upside from here. The problem is that it depends on franchisee adoption and the market starting to see the ~900 locations within the United States as possible.

I really see it, but only in the long term. Maybe after FY 2030. A good sign would be the return of net footprint growth.

Anyway, we'll continue to follow Dine Brands closely and I'll let you know if anything changes!

data.title: US Global Jets ETF: Why I Would Stay Away (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
This article is a follow-up analysis to the several airline reports that I have published in the past few days. Today, I will look at the U.S. Global Jets ETF (JETS), a fund that should reward (or punish) investors who choose to place a bet on the prospects of the U.S. airline business at large, without much concern for separating winners and losers within the peer group.

Below, I will explain my seemingly contradictory thesis. On the one hand, I believe that the US airline space is doing just fine as we cruise through the middle stages of the economic cycle, and I don't particularly see sizable risks on the horizon, other than the eventual short-term impact that events like the Middle East crisis could have on the industry. On the other hand, I don't necessarily believe that the tide will lift (or sink) all boats equally, which might make stock-picking in this sector more advisable.

About JETS

JETS is an ETF with AUM of about $700 million-plus that has been around since 2015. The fund trades an average volume of 4 million shares per day, according to Yahoo Finance, which amounts to roughly $100 million, making it look more like a trading vehicle rather than an investment vehicle. The management fee is a somewhat rich 60 bps per year.

As far as holdings are concerned, JETS distributed its assets across 55 airline-related holdings as of March 2026, although a mere ten of them received an allocation of just north of 60% of the total AUM. Maybe not a surprise, the Big 4 US airlines that include Southwest Airlines Co. (LUV), Delta Air Lines, Inc. (DAL), United Airlines Holdings, Inc. (UAL), and American Airlines Group Inc. (AAL) represent 42% of the fund (see pie chart below).

Finally, the largest non-US holding is Canadian airline Air Canada (ACDVF), at an allocation of 3.1%, and the largest non-North America holding is Spanish airport manager Aena S.M.E., S.A. (ANNSF), at an allocation of 1.1%.

Airline Space at Cruising Altitude

As I explained in my recent American Airlines article, airline revenues have been fairly well predicted on a 12-month lag, over the past three decades, by a few key leading economic indicators that speak to consumer behavior and the health of the so-called cyclical economy. The following indicators rank well on predictive power:

  • Initial unemployment claims.
  • Consumer expectations of future business conditions.
  • Building permits, to a lesser extent.

While the third one above has looked a bit soft since interest rates started to climb more decisively in early 2022, the broader picture is that the airline business seems to be in decent shape. To be clear, "decent" in this context refers more to a cruising-altitude state rather than the potential for substantially better capacity, occupancy, and per-unit prices ahead.

Above is a chart that plots projected US legacy airline revenue growth since the mid-1990s, based on the leading indicators above, against the historical reported figures (the chart "broke" a bit during the disruptive COVID-19 period). Notice how I currently expect the top line in this sector to grow by less than 5%, on average, over the next four quarters, suggesting business-as-usual conditions.

Aside from revenues, a dominating trend in the US airline sector since the pre-pandemic year of 2019 has been rising operating costs, excluding fuel, and compressing margins (see chart below). This has been due to skyrocketing personnel expenses during the high-inflation period of 2022-2025, aggravated by the need to ramp up airline capacity after the end of the COVID-19 lockdowns at virtually any cost, and a moderation in occupancy rates from the high levels of seven years ago.

Should the likes of Southwest Airlines, which is currently undergoing a business transformation process, manage to execute well on cost-saving initiatives as planned, some upside to earnings and to US airline stock prices could be captured without the need for materially higher passenger sales.

Picking Stocks Might Make More Sense

The above does not sound like a bearish or cautious view on the airline sector at all, which contradicts the title of this article. But I choose to set JETS aside and favor stock-picking in this case because, in my view and over a multi-year period, I believe that there will be relative winners and losers in the space.

The key criteria that I look at when choosing which companies to invest in (check out my recent coverage for a deeper dive) include the following:

  • Per-unit profitability: Here, I favor companies like DAL and UAL, as the former has consistently leveraged its strong hub position to pursue higher-margin domestic and international routes, and the latter has done a lot of heavy lifting in the past five years with a similar goal in mind.
  • Balance sheet robustness: I am much less comfortable investing in a heavily leveraged company within a space that is already sensitive to the economic cycles, which pushes me far away from the likes of AAL.
  • Proven business model: Airlines that have proven the efficacy of their business models over many years are more compelling to me than those that are still looking to reinvent themselves, which is the case with many low-cost carriers, including LUV.
  • Revenue diversification: Although a slightly less important item, I like to credit the likes of UAL and International Consolidated Airlines (ICAGY) for being well-positioned across geographically dispersed hubs that allow the companies to produce a more balanced mix of revenues from domestic vs. international destinations and economy vs. premium cabin passengers.

Therefore, I would be much more comfortable holding UAL, DAL, and ICAGY today than AAL, LUV, and even JETS itself. Therefore, I keep a "stay away" rating on the airline sector ETF, which is a downgrade from a few years ago, despite not necessarily being an outright bear on the space.

data.title: Iran War: One More Week Of Market Calm, Then Counting Down 100 Days To Tougher Times
data.description:
Investment thesis.

With the Iranian regime apparently having survived the initial shock of having a large swathe of its leadership now removed, it is now showing signs of resilience in fast decision-making and a willingness to turn this into a conflict of endurance. On one hand, it is completely outmatched militarily, and is under heavy attack, with little to show as an effective response in terms of inflicting military success. On the other hand, with its immediate and seemingly unwavering decision to close the Strait of Hormuz, it has effectively taken the global economy hostage.

As I shall explain, we probably have about a hundred days to find a way out of this before we risk a global economic implosion. At the same time, the market (SPY) will be largely aimless for another week or so, because if this war ends soon, it will have little impact from the market's perspective (not mentioning the human cost), therefore the market will still be pricing this possibility in. For now, I have the market as a hold, given my heavy allocation in oil and precious metals, which makes for a steady position going into this crisis. If this crisis lasts, I will switch to cautious, incremental bargain hunting.

Global Energy Markets are Facing Massive Disruption

It is day six of this conflict as I write this. With energy shipments through the Strait of Hormuz disrupted, the world probably lost about 100 million barrels of oil in supplies so far. It should be noted, however, that much of this oil will likely be found in regional storage facilities, and they will be tapped after the war. If it remains completely shut for the next 100 days, we could be looking at a loss of roughly 1 to 2 billion barrels in supplies. The world is also set to lose about 60 Bcm of LNG for the period. The latter should not produce widespread global effects, but rather some degree of shortages for some nations. India, for instance, has already taken measures to ration natural gas supplies headed for its industry.

I should clarify that my 100-day assessment is not for when we start experiencing economic turbulence. That is the approximate threshold where I see high chances of systemic risks, resulting from economic disruptions starting to create ripple effects, where even if we see an end to this war soon after this threshold is reached, it will be too late to prevent severe consequences from occurring, with wide-reaching effects potentially lasting for years.

Few Good Options to Stop this War

Just over two decades ago, the US confidently engaged in the Iraq regime change operation, however afterwards, the Sunni and Shia began to also attack the US presence. Iraq's neighbors ended up adding fuel the war by backing their own interests through various militias.

The current Iran regime change bears some similar hallmarks to what took place in Iraq. Assumptions that regime change would happen immediately after the removal of its leadership were clearly wrong. The Iranian regime is cornered, but not gone. It has no choice but to do what has always been feared would happen in the event of such a conflict. It decided to take the global economy hostage through the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, thus making the passage of time its best and arguably only true ally.

A land incursion to secure perhaps a few thousand square kilometers around the Strait of Hormuz might help to diminish the threat to shipping in the region, but it might not eliminate Iran's ability to target ships. There is no telling what unforeseen consequences such an operation might have in terms of losses; it will not be an easy decision to make.

There is also the option to widen the conflict by bringing in Iran's neighbors, such as Azerbaijan (via the arming of ethnic Azeris in Iran), or the UAE, which already stated a desire to retaliate against Iran's targeting of its territory due to US bases there. The problem with that is that Iran may likely respond by destroying the oil infrastructure of the UAE, as well as Azeri oil and gas infrastructure, if it gets involved, which then leaves us with oil supply disruptions that will last far beyond this war. Widening the conflict also has the potential for it to get out of control, and it could potentially last for years, much like the 2003 Iraq war did.

There is always the option of ending this war by simply calling it a mission accomplished, citing the destruction of missile stockpiles and production facilities as objectives achieved. If this is the path already chosen, we will likely see it happen in the next two weeks or so. The MSM will probably give the Trump administration a pass, in the name of the greater good, by not dwelling too much on the failure to achieve the original stated goal of regime change. The risk may be that Iran may unexpectedly not back down from its blockade of Strait of Hormuz, and set some demands, such as sanctions relief, before it does so.

Investment Implications

As the first week of war comes to an end, I executed trades amounting to about 2% of my portfolio, mostly as a cash-generating exercise, in expectation of bargain-hunting opportunities down the line. About a fifth of my portfolio is in cash. About a quarter of my stock portfolio is in oil & gas-related stocks, and those stocks are mostly up this week, but I do not deem the gains we have seen so far significant enough to warrant taking profits. The rest of the market has mostly responded aimlessly so far this week. No buying opportunity has emerged from my perspective thus far that makes it enticing enough within the context of where this conflict could be headed, with related risks and opportunities.

I expect more of the same from the markets going into next week. For this market to gain direction, we need physical proof of the consequences. That proof will arrive in about two weeks in the form of those oil and LNG tankers that were about to go through the Strait of Hormuz for pickup in the past few days, not delivering the volumes of oil and gas to the expected destinations. In the meantime, there ares still many vessels out there lining up for delivery.

Europeans, for instance, will likely come to the realization that as long as this conflict continues, they will have a hard time replenishing their depleted natural gas inventories for next winter. Their inventories are currently at the second-lowest level this decade.

With far fewer seaborne oil deliveries arriving to destined markets starting in a few weeks, the physical reality of inventory drawdowns will start to hit home. Petrochemical products such as fertilizers, liquid fuels, and chemical precursors to plastics will not make it to their destination either. This is where ripple effect calculations will enter the equation.

If farmers around the world do not have enough chemical fertilizer inputs, we can start thinking of food price inflation going into next year. Qatar, for instance, exports about one tenth of the world's urea. Inflation of energy prices, such as gasoline, will be felt in the economy immediately at the pump. Inflation in industrial and agricultural inputs will be felt down the line, setting us up for inflationary pressures for months or even years to come.

Roughly 100 days into this war, we are likely to see severe, but localized physical shortages throughout the global economy becoming widespread. Based on EIA data, countries like Italy, France, and Germany only have about 75-100 days' worth of oil & products stockpiled. Japan, which is heavily reliant on Middle Eastern oil imports, as well as LNG, will probably have to start aggressively rationing both oil and gas within about two months. It has an oil stockpile of 500 million barrels as of November of last year.

Even the US, although it is a net exporter of natural gas and only imports about 15% of its total oil and products needs, can suffer from a severe energy price shock, especially if measures are not taken to restrict exports. The cost of derived products such as fertilizers, plastics, and so on can rise, regardless of whether energy prices rise together with that of the global markets or not. The US Federal Reserve will probably feel compelled to act on rising inflation risks, in tandem with the world's other major central banks.

With these factors in mind, there are some broader considerations, as well as industry &and stock specific considerations that need to be weighed. It will take until next week at least for us to get a better sense of whether this conflict lasts or not. If a week from now, we can reasonably assume that both sides are entrenched for a long fight, then we need to consider the worst-case scenario, namely a conflict with no end in sight. This means that an oil supply shortfall of about 10-20 mb/d will have to be resolved through a global demand destruction event.

At around 100 days into this war, I expect we will see the markets starting to price in such an economic calamity, since actual physical shortages ranging from gasoline to chemicals will emerge all over the world. Oil producer stocks will probably ride this demand destruction event higher in its initial oil price spike phase. When we start to see a rebalancing of the supply/demand equation, they will probably sell off. The rebalancing could potentially lead to supply destruction overshoot, at which point oil prices could plunge dramatically, even as supplies continue to be shut in.

Most other market segments, except perhaps for gold and silver, are likely to see very steep selloffs as oil prices start to rise into demand destruction territory. With this in mind, I intend to be careful in pursuing bargains. True bargains may only arise when oil prices reach new all-time highs, beating the old record of 2008.

Some of the specific stocks I am looking at all have particular profiles that have the potential to make them relative winners within this context.

Equinor ASA(EQNR) is well-positioned to take advantage of oil and natural gas prices potentially rising at a faster pace in Europe than elsewhere, such as in the US and China. It is Europe's largest natural gas provider, and it also produces most of its oil in Norway, which is mostly sold on the European market.

Dow Inc. (DOW) has the potential to benefit from being a mostly US-based chemicals company. Natural gas prices are likely to diverge even more than they already are between the US market and more expensive markets such as Europe. Dow Inc. could potentially become a major chemical products exporter to markets that are experiencing severe natural gas supply disruptions as a result of Middle Eastern LNG exports being shut in. Those market share gains could become permanent if the war is prolonged.

I am somewhat positive on traditional defensive stocks such as Kimberly-Clark Corporation (KMB) or The Kraft-Heinz Corporation (KHC). Their input costs should increase significantly, but at the same time, I believe that they have enough pricing power to pass on at least some of the cost increases to the consumer. These are stocks I am looking to hold through this crisis.

Health stocks such as Abbott Laboratories (ABT) may fare alright, as long as this conflict ends at some point within the next few months. Beyond that, we could potentially reach a point where enough people would be left to make increasingly tough choices on whether they buy food or spend on health care needs, to the point where hospitals, labs, and other facilities may stop buying supplies.

I continue to see gold and silver as important safe-haven assets, despite what the first trading days since the start of this war suggest.

As I already stated, I would not put a lot of stock into making assumptions on market direction based on the first few days of trading since the war started. I am looking to buy gold and silver stocks on a sizable dip. I already have a very robust precious metals position in my portfolio, which is why I am not rushing to buy as this war unfolds.

Risks to my Thesis

My main strategy of mostly staying put for the first two weeks of this war might lead to many opportunities missed, as well as a chance to unload stocks that could face dramatic selloffs in the coming weeks. If the war ends very soon, I may miss out on taking profits on my sizable oil and gas positions. There is also a high probability that we will start seeing the broader stock market sell off dramatically starting next week, so it may make some sense to reduce one's positions in certain stocks now, rather than later, if one is looking to reposition in expectation of a broad market selloff.

Conclusions

The latest news is that Iran switched from a blanket closure of the Strait to a selective access system, where ships signaling that they operate for China, for instance, are given a pass. If this is the case, and perhaps only ships intended for supplying US-friendly states like Japan or most of Europe are being stopped, the net effect on the global economy might be a significant delay of my 100-day timeline to absolute disaster.

At the same time, it signals that Iran has more than just a sporadic ability to threaten anything that moves through the Strait. If this is the case, we could be in for a very long standoff, and whether we reach the severe global economic crisis point in 100 days or 200 days, we are headed there, and as investors, we need to prepare accordingly. Until we see a bit more clarity on whether we are in for a long war or not, there is a case to be made for not rushing to trade just yet, even though it does come with some risk. We should get more clarity next week.
